content:
# 1 John Chapter 5 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-06

---

### v.6 — "came by water and blood" vs. "was revealed by his baptism in water and by shedding his blood on the cross"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the [one] having come through water and blood" | ho elthōn di' hydatos kai haimatos |
| LOGOS | "he who came by water and blood" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "was revealed as God's Son by his baptism in water and by shedding his blood on the cross" | Interpretive expansion: adds "baptism," "shedding," "on the cross" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says "water and blood" — two words, deliberately compressed. PNEUMA expands this into a full narrative: "baptism in water" and "shedding his blood on the cross." While the interpretation (baptism and crucifixion) is widely accepted, the author's compressed phrasing is deliberate. The very brevity forces the reader to think about what "water and blood" mean — and scholars still debate whether water refers to baptism, birth (John 3:5), or the water from Jesus' side (John 19:34).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA expands the compressed into the explicit.

---

### v.19 — "the whole world lies in the power of the evil one" vs. "the world around us is under the control of the evil one"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the whole world lies in the evil one" | ho kosmos holos en tō ponērō keitai |
| LOGOS | "the whole world lies in the power of the evil one" | Adds "power" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "the world around us is under the control of the evil one" | "Around us" added; "control" for keitai |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek says the world "lies in" (keitai en) the evil one — a spatial metaphor of rest or immersion. LOGOS adds "power" to clarify the meaning (lying in the evil one's power). PNEUMA's "around us" is not in the text and "control" replaces the more passive "lies in" with active domination. The author's image is of the world passively resting in the evil one's sphere — not actively controlled by him.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.21 — "keep yourselves from idols" vs. "keep away from anything that might take God's place in your hearts"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "guard yourselves from idols" | phylaxate heauta apo tōn eidōlōn |
| LOGOS | "keep yourselves from idols" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "keep away from anything that might take God's place in your hearts" | Interprets "idols" broadly |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says eidōla — "idols." The word means images, false gods, or objects of false worship. PNEUMA's expansive rendering ("anything that might take God's place in your hearts") is a valid application but not a translation. The author's bluntness is the point: after five chapters of nuanced theology, the final word is the simplest possible: no idols. Expanding it dilutes the impact.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA interprets rather than translates.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA expands compressed language | 1 | Interpretive expansion of "water and blood" (v.6) |
| PNEUMA adds spatial/interpretive glosses | 1 | "Around us" and "control" (v.19) |
| PNEUMA interprets rather than translates | 1 | "Anything that might take God's place" for "idols" (v.21) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 5's most significant disagreement is v.6 — the expansion of "water and blood" into explicit baptism and crucifixion references. The author's compressed language is deliberately open to multiple interpretations, and PNEUMA closes this openness. The final verse (v.21) illustrates the broader pattern: PNEUMA interprets where the author states, losing the rhetorical force of directness.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
